A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I see there are gray squares at the bottom (rows 58-59). These look like buttons. There are 7 of them at columns 10, 17, 24, 31, 38, 45, 52. These correspond to the 7 color positions! Maybe I need to click these buttons to submit/confirm each dot's color?
Looking at the layout:
- The 7 gray squares at the bottom might be "confirm" buttons for each position
- Or they might be a progress indicator
Let me look at the green bar at row 53 - it goes from col 0 to col 40 (g=gray, 41 pixels) and then col 41 to 63 (G=dark gray, 23 pixels). This looks like a progress bar showing 41/64 completed.
The 7 gray squares at the bottom (58-59) could be buttons to confirm the solution. Let me try clicking one of them, or maybe clicking the green bar area.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the valid actions: SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7. ACTION7 gave an error. Let me check what other action names might work. Maybe there's an ENTER or SUBMIT action?
Actually, looking at the bottom row squares more carefully - they're at positions that align with the 7 dots. Maybe I need to click them in order to confirm each dot? Or maybe clicking them does something else.
Let me try clicking the first gray square at (58, 10) to see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: I see 7 gray squares at the bottom that align with the 7 dot positions. Let me try clicking one.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking the first gray square at bottom
predict(expect_change=True, note='Click bottom gray square to confirm/submit')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 10}])

print(f"Result: {last_action_result}")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
# Check bottom squares
for n in nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    min_r = min(p[0] for p in b)
    if min_r >= 53:
        print(f"  Bottom: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bounds=({min_r},{min(p[1] for p in b)})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
